title: Model Selection: Train / Validation / Test and Avoiding Lucky Winners
overview: How to evaluate and choose the best machine-learning model so its measured performance reflects how it will behave on new, unseen data. Covers the train/validation/test split, converting model scores to predictions, the multiple-comparison (lucky model) problem, and the standard workflow to select, verify, and finalize a model.

key_concepts.title: Training set
key_concepts.description: The subset used to fit model parameters. The model learns patterns from this data.
bullet_points: Used only for fitting models (weights, tree splits, etc.)
Should not be used for any model selection decisions

key_concepts.title: Validation set
key_concepts.description: A held-out subset used to compare different model choices and tune hyperparameters.
bullet_points: Used to estimate out-of-sample performance for model selection
If reused repeatedly, may cause optimistic bias (overfitting to the validation set)

key_concepts.title: Test set
key_concepts.description: A final held-out subset used once to estimate the performance of the selected model.
bullet_points: Only used after model selection is finished
Gives an unbiased estimate of how the chosen model will perform in production

key_concepts.title: Multiple comparison / lucky model problem
key_concepts.description: When many models are evaluated on the same validation set, some models will appear unusually good by chance.
bullet_points: Statistical phenomenon: testing many hypotheses increases chance of false positives
A model can 'get lucky' and fit idiosyncrasies of the validation subset

key_concepts.title: Model selection workflow
key_concepts.description: A disciplined three-stage split and evaluation process to pick a model and validate its real-world performance.
bullet_points: Split data into train, validation, and test sets
Use train to fit candidate models, validation to compare them, test to verify the final choice
Optionally retrain the chosen model on train+validation and then evaluate on test

blocks.content: Models are evaluated to estimate how they will perform when deployed on future data. Because we cannot see the future, we mimic deployment by withholding parts of our available data and treating them as unseen. The goal of model selection is to pick the model that will generalize best to new data, not merely the model that performs best on the data used for training.

items: Training: fit model parameters using training data (X_train, y_train).
Validation: compare candidate models using held-out validation data (X_val, y_val).
Test: final unbiased check using held-out test data (X_test, y_test).

blocks.content: A common pattern is to split the dataset into three non-overlapping subsets: train (e.g., 60%), validation (e.g., 20%), and test (e.g., 20%). The exact ratios are flexible and depend on dataset size.

blocks.diagram:
flowchart TB
    A["Full dataset"] --> B["Split into: Train, Validation, Test"]
    B --> C["Train (fit candidate models)"]
    C --> D["Apply models to Validation -> Select best model"]
    D --> E["Final model applied to Test (one-time evaluation)"]
    E --> F["If final model satisfactory: deploy"]

items: Never use validation or test examples for training while comparing models.
The test set must remain untouched until model selection is complete.
After selection, you may retrain the selected model on train+validation to use more data for the final model, but still evaluate only on the test set.

detailed_explanations.title: From model scores (probabilities) to predictions and accuracy

blocks.content: Many classifiers output scores or probabilities (for example, P(spam) = 0.7). To compute discrete predictions and metrics like accuracy, apply a decision rule (threshold). Common default: threshold = 0.5 for binary classification.

items: Probability -> Prediction: predict 1 (positive) if score >= threshold else 0.
Accuracy = number of correct predictions / total examples.
Example: predictions and actual labels can be compared row-wise to compute accuracy or other metrics (precision, recall, AUC, etc.).

blocks.diagram:
flowchart TB
    S["Model outputs probabilities"] --> T["Apply threshold (e.g., 0.5)"]
    T --> P["Discrete predictions"]
    P --> M["Compare with ground truth -> Metrics (accuracy, etc.)"]

detailed_explanations.title: Multiple comparisons and the 'lucky model' example

blocks.content: If you fit and evaluate many candidate models on the same validation set, one model may appear to perform extremely well purely due to random alignment with that specific validation subset. This is the multiple-comparison problem: as the number of comparisons grows, so does the chance of a false positive (a model that looks good by luck).

Illustration (coin-flip analogy): treat each model as a random coin flip; if one coin happens to reproduce the validation labels exactly, its apparent accuracy will be 100% on that validation set while being meaningless for other unseen data.

items: Evaluation on a single validation split can be misleading when many candidate models/hyperparameters are tried.
A model that fits peculiarities of the validation set will not generalize.
This motivates holding out a separate test set that is not consulted during selection.

blocks.content: A durable procedure to select a model while guarding against lucky winners:

items: 1) Split data into train / validation / test (e.g., 60/20/20).
2) Using only the training data, fit each candidate model (or fit on training + internal CV folds for hyperparameter tuning).
3) Evaluate each candidate model on the validation set; record metrics (accuracy, precision, etc.).
4) Select the best model based on validation performance (and other considerations like complexity, latency).
5) To confirm selection, evaluate the chosen model once on the test set. This gives an unbiased performance estimate.
6) Optionally retrain the chosen model on train + validation to produce a final model and then evaluate on the test set again (test remains the same).

blocks.diagram:
flowchart TB
    A["Split: Train, Val, Test"] --> B["Fit candidate models on Train"]
    B --> C["Evaluate each model on Val -> Compare metrics"]
    C --> D["Select best model"]
    D --> E["Evaluate best model on Test (one-time)"]
    E --> F["If acceptable, retrain on Train+Val for final model"]
    F --> G["Final check on Test -> Deploy"]

detailed_explanations.title: Practical note: reuse of validation set for final training

blocks.content: After the validation-driven selection is complete, the validation set need not be wasted. Final practice is to combine training and validation sets to train the final selected model (more training data generally improves model performance). The test set remains the only held-out set for assessing the finalized model.

items: Combine train + validation -> train final model (same model architecture and hyperparameters selected earlier).
Evaluate final model once on the test set to confirm performance.
Do not re-use the test set for further selection or tuning; doing so invalidates its role as an unbiased estimator.

detailed_explanations.title: Compact pseudocode for model selection loop

blocks.code:
## Pseudocode (high-level)
# Assumes dataset already split into train, val, test
candidates = [modelA, modelB, modelC]  # different model types or hyperparameter configs
val_scores = {}
for model in candidates:
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)            # fit using training data only
    probs = model.predict_proba(X_val)     # or model.predict(X_val) depending on API
    preds = (probs >= 0.5).astype(int)     # convert to discrete predictions
    val_scores[model] = accuracy(y_val, preds)

best_model = argmax(val_scores)
# Validate best model on test set (one-time evaluation)
best_model.fit(X_train, y_train)  # if you want to reproduce the exact selected model trained only on train
# Optionally retrain on train+val for final production model
best_model.fit(concat(X_train, X_val), concat(y_train, y_val))
test_preds = (best_model.predict_proba(X_test) >= 0.5).astype(int)
final_test_score = accuracy(y_test, test_preds)
print("Final test score:", final_test_score)

key_takeaway: Always split your data into train, validation, and test when comparing multiple models to prevent selecting a model that is good only by chance.
Use the validation set for selection and the test set for a one-time unbiased estimate of the chosen model's performance.
After selecting the model, you may retrain on train+validation before deploying, but continue to rely on the test set for the final assessment.
project_ideas: Implement a simple model selection pipeline on a small labeled dataset (e.g., spam classification). Compare logistic regression, decision tree, and random forest using a 60/20/20 split. Report validation and test metrics and retrain the best model on train+validation.

Simulate the multiple-comparison problem: generate synthetic data and evaluate many random classifiers on the same validation split to demonstrate how a random model can appear to be the best by chance. Then show how a held-out test set reveals the true performance.
Build an experiment that varies the train/val/test ratios and measures how stable validation-based selection is (i.e., how often the selected model also wins on the test set).

timestamps.concept: Introduction to modeling step and model selection
timestamps.learning_value: Sets the stage for why model selection is required; clarifies difference between trying different model families and selecting based on data.
Shows the visual idea of withholding a portion of data as validation (mimicking future unseen data).
timestamps.visual_to_capture: Slide or drawing showing the dataset with a highlighted 80% training block and 20% validation block (the validation block labeled as hidden).
timestamps.screenshot_reason: A clear visual of the withheld validation block helps learners connect the abstract idea of 'future data' to the concrete step of hiding part of the current dataset.
timestamps.suggested_notes_location: Detailed Explanations and Examples > Train / Validation / Test split (concept + flow)
timestamps.suggested_caption: Illustration: hide 20% of data as validation to simulate unseen future data

timestamps.concept: Converting probabilities to predictions and computing accuracy
timestamps.learning_value: Walks through converting model probability outputs into binary predictions and comparing them to true labels to compute accuracy.
timestamps.visual_to_capture: The table showing model probabilities for each example, the applied threshold, resulting binary predictions, and comparison to true labels (correct/incorrect marks).
timestamps.screenshot_reason: A single-table screenshot ties the numeric probabilities to discrete decisions and the resulting correctness counts, making the metric computation tangible.
timestamps.suggested_notes_location: Detailed Explanations and Examples > From model scores (probabilities) to predictions and accuracy
timestamps.suggested_caption: Probabilities -> threshold -> discrete predictions -> correctness check

timestamps.learning_value: Introduces the coin-flip analogy illustrating how random models can appear to perform well on a single validation split by chance.
timestamps.visual_to_capture: The sequence of validation labels and several coin-flip model prediction sequences showing varying accuracies, culminating with an exact match.
timestamps.screenshot_reason: Visualizing multiple random prediction sequences side-by-side demonstrates how one can align perfectly with the validation labels purely by chance.
timestamps.suggested_notes_location: Detailed Explanations and Examples > Multiple comparisons and the 'lucky model' example
timestamps.suggested_caption: Multiple random prediction sequences; one happens to match validation labels exactly

timestamps.learning_value: Formalizes the practice of keeping a separate test set in addition to the validation set to detect lucky winners.
timestamps.visual_to_capture: Diagram or slide that partitions the full dataset into three labeled, non-overlapping parts: Train (e.g., 60%), Validation (20%), Test (20%).
timestamps.screenshot_reason: Students often conflate validation and test; a clear partition diagram reinforces the distinct roles of each subset.
timestamps.suggested_notes_location: Detailed Explanations and Examples > Train / Validation / Test split (concept + flow)
timestamps.suggested_caption: Three-way data split: Train, Validation, Test (non-overlapping)

timestamps.concept: Applying selected model to test data for final check
timestamps.learning_value: Shows the final verification step: once a best model is selected on validation, evaluate it on the test set to check whether it was a lucky winner.
timestamps.suggested_notes_location: Detailed Explanations and Examples > Standard model selection workflow (step-by-step)

timestamps.concept: Retrain on train + validation for final model
timestamps.learning_value: Explains how to use the validation set as training data after selection to improve the final model before deployment, without touching the test set.
timestamps.visual_to_capture: Diagram showing train and validation being merged into a larger training set, then training the final model and evaluating on test.
timestamps.screenshot_reason: This visual clarifies the final-data-reuse step that often confuses learners: validation can become training after selection, but test must remain untouched.
timestamps.suggested_notes_location: Detailed Explanations and Examples > Practical note: reuse of validation set for final training
timestamps.suggested_caption: Combine Train + Validation -> train final model -> evaluate on Test
